title: How much does public EV charging cost?
description: Public rapid chargers average about 79p per kWh in mid 2026 and slower public units about 54p. See what that means per charge, per mile, and how to pay less.
blurb: Rapid chargers average 79p per kWh, ten times a cheap home tariff. What a charge costs and how to pay less.
lede: No other electricity you buy has a price spread like this. The same kilowatt hour that costs around 5.5p on the cheapest overnight EV tariff at home can cost 79p or more at a motorway rapid charger, a fourteen-fold difference for an identical product. If you drive an electric car, understanding that spread is worth hundreds of pounds a year, because where you plug in matters far more than how far you drive. This guide sets out what public charging actually costs in mid 2026, why the prices are what they are, and the practical ways to keep your average down even if you rely on public chargers most of the time.
short_answer: In mid 2026, public rapid and ultra-rapid chargers in the UK average around 79p per kWh, while slower public chargers (lamp post, car park and destination units) average around 54p per kWh, according to Zapmap. That works out at roughly 15p to 23p per mile, compared with about 7.5p per mile charging at home on the Ofgem-capped rate of 26.11p per kWh, and around 2p per mile on a cheap overnight EV tariff. A typical 10 to 80% rapid top-up on a 60 kWh car costs about £33.

sections.h2: What public charging costs in mid 2026
sections.html:
Public chargers in the UK price by the kilowatt hour, just like your home supply, so comparing them is straightforward once you know the going rates. Zapmap, which tracks prices across the UK networks, puts the mid 2026 average at about 79p per kWh for rapid and ultra-rapid chargers (50 kW and above, the kind you find at motorway services and charging hubs) and about 54p per kWh for slower public units such as lamp post chargers, kerbside posts and 7 to 22 kW car park chargers.

Here is what those rates mean in practice for a typical car. The per-mile figures assume 3.5 miles per kWh, a reasonable mid-size EV average, and the top-up column shows a 10 to 80% session on a 60 kWh battery, which is 42 kWh delivered.

Where you chargeTypical price per kWhCost per mile42 kWh top-up
Home, off-peak EV tariff7p2p£2.94
Home, standard capped rate26.11p7.5p£10.97
Public slow and fast (lamp post, car park)54p15.4p£22.68
Public rapid and ultra-rapid79p22.6p£33.18

Public prices are Zapmap network averages, mid 2026. Home standard rate is 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Per-mile figures assume 3.5 miles per kWh.

Prices vary a lot around those averages. Some ultra-rapid hubs charge 85p or more at peak times, while a handful of networks offer overnight rapid rates in the 50s. Slow kerbside units run from the high 30s to the 60s depending on the operator and the council deal behind them. Always check the live price in the network's app or on the unit before you plug in, because the gap between the cheapest and dearest charger on the same street can be 30p per kWh.

sections.h2: Why public charging costs three times the home rate
sections.html:
A 79p kilowatt hour sounds like profiteering next to the 26.11p you pay at home, but most of the gap has structural causes.

  • VAT is four times higher. Domestic electricity carries 5% VAT, but electricity sold through public chargers is taxed at the standard 20% rate. That difference alone adds roughly 10p per kWh at rapid-charger prices, and campaigners have pushed for years to equalise it. Check the current VAT treatment on GOV.UK if you are reading this later, as the rules may change.
  • The hardware is expensive. A single 150 kW ultra-rapid unit plus its grid connection can cost an operator a six-figure sum to install. That capital has to be recovered across every kilowatt hour sold, and many sites still sit idle for much of the day.
  • Commercial electricity costs more. Operators buy power on commercial contracts with capacity and peak-demand charges that domestic customers never see, and a rapid hub drawing hundreds of kilowatts pays heavily for that grid capacity.
  • Running costs. Site rent, maintenance, card payment fees, customer support lines and back-office software all sit on top of the electricity itself.

None of this makes an expensive charger cheap, but it explains why public prices have stayed high even as wholesale electricity has settled. You are paying for speed, grid capacity and convenience, not just energy. The full home-side picture, including what a wallbox session really costs, is covered in our guide to charging an EV at home.

sections.h2: Work out your own cost per charge and per mile
sections.html:
Two simple sums tell you everything you need at any charger.

Cost per charge

Multiply the kilowatt hours you expect to add by the price per kWh. Adding 30% to 80% on a 75 kWh battery means 37.5 kWh, so at 79p that session costs £29.63. Public chargers bill you for energy delivered to the car, so the meter on the unit is what you pay for.

Cost per mile

Divide the price per kWh by your car's efficiency in miles per kWh. At 54p per kWh and 3.5 miles per kWh, you are paying 15.4p per mile. A smaller, efficient EV doing 4.2 miles per kWh pays only 12.9p per mile at the same charger, while a heavy SUV doing 2.8 miles per kWh pays 19.3p. Your car's trip computer shows your real figure, and it drops noticeably in winter, when the same money buys fewer miles.

Keep those two numbers in your head and every charger price instantly translates into something meaningful. Our EV charging cost calculator guide walks through the method step by step with worked examples for different battery sizes.

sections.h2: How public charging compares with petrol
sections.html:
This is where the numbers get uncomfortable for anyone who relies entirely on rapid chargers. With petrol at around 159p per litre in mid 2026, a petrol car returning 45 mpg costs about 16p per mile in fuel. Against that:

  • Rapid charging at 79p per kWh costs about 22.6p per mile, which is more expensive than petrol.
  • Slower public charging at 54p per kWh costs about 15.4p per mile, roughly level with petrol.
  • Home charging at the capped 26.11p rate costs about 7.5p per mile, less than half the petrol figure.
  • An off-peak EV tariff at 7p per kWh costs about 2p per mile, an eighth of the petrol cost.

What matters is your blend. Over 8,000 miles a year a typical EV uses about 2,290 kWh. Charged entirely on rapids, that is about £1,810 a year, more than the £1,280 or so the equivalent petrol car would burn. But a realistic mix of 80% home off-peak charging and 20% rapid charging on longer trips comes to roughly £490, saving nearly £800 a year against petrol. The full comparison, including servicing and depreciation, is in our guide to EV running costs versus petrol.

The lesson is blunt. An EV with no access to home or workplace charging, fuelled only at rapid hubs, can cost more per mile than a petrol car. An EV that does most of its charging on a cheap overnight tariff is dramatically cheaper. The economics live or die on where you plug in.

sections.h2: How to pay less at public chargers
sections.html:
Even if you cannot charge at home, the gap between a careless public charging habit and a careful one is large.

  • Use the slowest charger that fits your stop. Speed is what you pay a premium for. If the car is parked for three hours anyway, a 7 kW car park charger at 50p per kWh beats a rapid at 79p for exactly the same electricity.
  • Look at network membership schemes. Several large networks offer a monthly subscription that cuts the per-kWh price, typically by 15p to 25p. If you rapid-charge more than once or twice a month, the fee usually pays for itself. Compare the saving per kWh against your monthly volume rather than trusting the headline.
  • Charge off-peak where the network prices by time. Some rapid networks now charge less overnight or outside busy windows. The same logic that makes home tariffs cheap at night applies on the road; our guide to the best time to charge an electric car covers both sides.
  • Hunt out destination charging. Some supermarkets, gyms, hotels and workplaces still offer free or heavily subsidised charging while you are there. It is rarer than it was, but a free 7 kW hour while you shop is 7 kWh you did not pay 79p for.
  • Stop at 80% on rapids. Charging slows sharply above 80%, so the last fifth of the battery takes disproportionately long, and several networks add overstay or idle fees once your session ends. Unplug and move on.
  • Watch for pre-authorisation holds. Contactless payment usually places a temporary hold of £25 to £45 on your card before the session. It is released, not charged, but it can sting on a low balance.

If you have on-street parking only, lamp post and kerbside chargers in the 40s and 50s per kWh are usually your cheapest regular option, and some councils now offer residential charging schemes. Our guide to EV charging without a driveway covers the options in detail.

Public EV charging in mid 2026 costs about 54p per kWh on slower units and about 79p per kWh on rapids, which is two to three times the capped home rate of 26.11p and over ten times a cheap overnight EV tariff. Per mile, rapid charging is dearer than petrol; home charging is far cheaper than both. Treat rapids as the motorway-services coffee of electricity, fine occasionally, ruinous as a habit, and push as much of your charging as possible onto the cheapest source available to you. If you can charge at home at all, getting the right tariff matters more than anything you do on the public network, and our guide to EV electricity tariffs explains how those deals work and what to look for.

faq.q: How much does it cost to fully charge an EV at a public rapid charger?
faq.a: At the mid 2026 average of 79p per kWh, a typical 10 to 80% rapid session on a 60 kWh battery (42 kWh delivered) costs about £33. Charging the same battery from empty to full would cost about £47, though in practice almost nobody rapid-charges to 100% because the rate slows sharply above 80%.

faq.q: Is public EV charging more expensive than petrol?
faq.a: Rapid charging usually is. At 79p per kWh and 3.5 miles per kWh, you pay about 22.6p per mile, against roughly 16p per mile for a 45 mpg petrol car with fuel at 159p per litre. Slower public chargers at around 54p per kWh are roughly level with petrol, while home charging is far cheaper at 7.5p per mile on the capped rate and around 2p per mile on an off-peak EV tariff.

faq.q: Why is VAT higher at public chargers than at home?
faq.a: Domestic electricity is taxed at 5% VAT, but electricity sold through public charge points is treated as a standard-rated supply and carries 20% VAT. That difference adds roughly 10p per kWh at rapid-charger prices. The rules have been challenged repeatedly, so check the current VAT treatment on GOV.UK.

faq.q: Are there extra fees on top of the price per kWh?
faq.a: Sometimes. Contactless payments usually involve a temporary pre-authorisation hold of £25 to £45 that is released after the session. Many networks add overstay or idle fees if you leave the car connected after charging finishes, and a few sites add parking charges. Per-kWh pricing covers the electricity itself on almost all UK networks.

faq.q: Do network subscriptions actually save money?
faq.a: They can if you rapid-charge regularly. A typical scheme charges a monthly fee in exchange for a per-kWh discount of 15p to 25p. Divide the monthly fee by the discount to find your break-even: a £8 fee against a 20p discount pays off once you buy more than 40 kWh a month on that network, roughly one decent rapid session.
